3.happening: self.val = val stores the integer on this node.
3.why: Each ListNode holds its value in self.val; this is set during construction for every node we create.
3.invariant: This node's val field is now populated with the integer value.

4.happening: self.next = nxt sets the next pointer (None by default).
4.why: The linked list is formed through .next pointers; None marks the tail of the list.
4.invariant: This node's next field is initialized — nodes are disconnected until .next is explicitly set.

8.happening: We receive head as a Python list (e.g. [1,2,6,3,4,5,6]) and keep it as vals. If the input is empty we use [].
8.why: The tracer passes inputs as plain Python values; we convert them to real ListNode objects next.
8.invariant: vals holds the raw integer values we will turn into a linked list.

9.happening: Create one ListNode for each value in vals.
9.why: We need real nodes with .next pointers so we can do genuine pointer manipulation — not just array slicing.
9.invariant: nodes_list is a flat Python list of disconnected nodes.

10.happening: Wire up each node's .next to point to the following node.
10.why: A linked list is built by chaining .next pointers; without this step the nodes are isolated.
10.invariant: After the loop, nodes_list[0].next → nodes_list[1] → … → nodes_list[-1].next = None.

11.happening: real_head is assigned the first node (or None for an empty list).
11.why: We need to know where the list begins before we can attach the dummy sentinel.
11.invariant: real_head points to the first real node, or is None.

12.happening: Create a dummy node (value 0) as a sentinel before the real list.
12.why: If the head node itself needs to be deleted, we would lose the head pointer. The dummy node means current.next is always the node we are evaluating — the head is just another next pointer.
12.invariant: dummy is a stable anchor; it is never removed.

13.happening: Connect dummy.next → real_head so the full list starts right after dummy.
13.why: Now dummy sits just before the first real node — any node, including the head, can be deleted by the same pointer-skip logic.

14.why: We always examine current.next (the candidate for deletion), not current itself. Starting at dummy means we can handle head deletion without special cases.
14.invariant: current.next is the next node to inspect.

15.happening: Build the visual nodes array — each node gets an id and a label showing its value.
15.why: The linkedList renderer reads from this array to draw the nodes on screen.
15.invariant: nodes mirrors the current live nodes in the list.

16.happening: Build the visual links array — each entry is one → arrow between consecutive nodes.
16.why: The renderer uses this to draw the directional arrows connecting nodes.
16.invariant: links mirrors the current live arrows in the list.

17.happening: changed_links starts empty — no arrows have been rewritten yet.
17.why: The renderer highlights rewritten arrows in a special color; nothing has changed yet.
17.invariant: changed_links is empty until a node is deleted.

18.happening: node_ids is a bookkeeping list [0, 1, 2, …] with one entry per node.
18.why: Needed to build id_map on the next line.
18.invariant: node_ids aligns with nodes_list by index.

19.happening: id_map maps each node's Python memory address (id()) to its visual id number.
19.why: When we follow .next pointers to find which node comes after a deleted one, we need to translate Python objects back to visual ids.
19.invariant: id_map[id(nodeX)] == X for every node X.

20.happening: The dummy node gets visual id -1 so the pointer can render on it.
20.why: -1 is a sentinel id that the renderer recognises as the dummy/pre-head node.
20.invariant: id_map is complete for all nodes including dummy.

21.happening: curr_id starts at -1 — the dummy node's visual id.
21.why: current is sitting at dummy, so its visual id should be -1.
21.invariant: curr_id always equals the visual id of current.

22.happening: Check: is current.next non-null? If yes, there is a node ahead to inspect.
22.why: We stop as soon as current.next is None — there is nothing further to remove.
22.invariant: When the loop is entered, current.next is always a real node.

23.happening: Refresh curr_id to the visual id of current's node.
23.why: current may have moved on a previous iteration; we re-read its id so the visual pointer stays in sync.

24.happening: next_id = visual id of current.next — the node we are about to judge.
24.why: We need this id to update the visual if we delete current.next.
24.invariant: next_id points to the candidate node.

25.happening: current.next.val equals val — this node is marked for deletion!
25.why: The algorithm's job is to skip every node whose value matches val.
25.invariant: current.next will be bypassed; current stays put.

25.happening: current.next.val does NOT equal val — this node survives.
25.why: Only nodes with the target value are removed; all others remain in the list.
25.invariant: current will advance past this surviving node.

26.happening: Save the visual id of the node about to be deleted as skip_id.
26.why: We need skip_id to filter this node out of the nodes and links arrays after the relink.
26.invariant: skip_id is the id of the doomed node.

27.happening: Find after_id — the visual id of the node that will become current.next once the deletion is done.
27.why: We need after_id to draw the new relinked arrow from current to the node after the deleted one.
27.invariant: after_id is None if the deleted node was the last in the list.

28.happening: Bypass: current.next now skips the matched node and points directly to the node after it.
28.why: This is the heart of the deletion — by never following the deleted node, it is unreachable from dummy onward and effectively removed.
28.invariant: The deleted node is now orphaned and will be removed from the visual.

29.happening: Remove the deleted node's arrows from the links array.
29.why: Any arrow pointing into or out of the deleted node must disappear from the visual.
29.invariant: links no longer contains any arrow connected to skip_id.

30.happening: Remove the deleted node itself from the nodes array.
30.why: The node should vanish from the visual — no box, no label.
30.invariant: nodes no longer contains the deleted node.

31.happening: Record a new arrow from current to after_id in changed_links.
31.why: The renderer will highlight this new arrow to show the list has been relinked.
31.invariant: changed_links contains exactly the new arrow.

32.happening: Store the relinked arrow in changed_links (empty list if after_id is None — deleted node was the tail).
32.why: The renderer reads changed_links each step to flash newly-created arrows in a highlight color.
32.invariant: changed_links is either a single-entry list with the new arrow, or empty.

33.happening: current.next.val ≠ val — advance current to current.next.
33.why: This node stays; we move current forward to inspect the next one.
33.invariant: current has moved one step closer to the end.

34.happening: curr_id is updated to reflect current's new position.
34.why: Keep the visual pointer in sync with the actual current pointer.

35.happening: Clear changed_links — no arrow was rewritten this step.
35.why: We didn't delete anything, so no new arrows need highlighting.
35.invariant: changed_links is empty for this step.

36.happening: Reset changed_links to empty — no link was rewritten because we advanced without deleting.
36.why: The renderer highlights rewritten arrows; since no node was removed, there are no new arrows to flash.
36.invariant: changed_links is empty for this step.

37.happening: Start collecting surviving values into result_vals.
37.why: The result must be the values of the remaining nodes in order.
37.invariant: result_vals is empty; node starts at the new head.

38.happening: node starts at dummy.next — the new head of the list.
38.why: dummy.next is the first surviving node after all deletions.
38.invariant: We are about to read the surviving nodes in order.

39.invariant: result_vals grows by one entry per surviving node.

40.happening: Advance to the next surviving node.
40.why: Continue walking until we reach the end (None).
40.invariant: node advances one step through the remaining list.

41.happening: Assign result_vals to result so the resultSpec can display it.
41.why: The platform looks for a variable named 'result' to show in the result chip.
41.invariant: result holds the complete ordered list of surviving values.

42.happening: Assign result_vals to result so the platform can locate the final answer.
42.why: The resultSpec looks for a variable named 'result'; renaming here ensures it is found.
42.invariant: result holds the complete ordered list of surviving values.

43.happening: Return result — the ordered list of node values that survived.
43.why: The problem asks for the new head; we return the values of the remaining list in order.
43.invariant: Every node with value == val has been removed.

init.happening: Building the linked list and initialising the dummy sentinel.
init.why: We convert the input array to real nodes, wire them up, and place a dummy node before the head so every deletion is handled uniformly.
init.invariant: dummy → head → … → None; current is at dummy.

loop.why: We continue as long as there is a next node to evaluate.
loop.invariant: current.next is the candidate node for this iteration.

check.why: Only nodes matching val should be removed.
check.invariant: current.next either matches and will be skipped, or doesn't match and will be kept.

update.happening: Bypassing the matched node and updating the visual.
update.why: Removing the node by relinking current.next past it.
update.invariant: The deleted node is gone from the visual; the list is one node shorter.

move.happening: Advancing current one step to the right.
move.why: The current node survived; we move on to check the next candidate.
move.invariant: current has advanced; current.next is the new candidate.

return.happening: Returning the list of surviving values.
return.why: All val-matching nodes have been removed; the remaining values are the answer.
return.invariant: result contains exactly the node values that did not equal val, in original order.
